senses.gloss: August (eighth month of the Gregorian and Julian calendars)
to incur, catch, be left with

etymology.text: From Classical Syriac ܐܒ (ʾāḇ), ultimately from Akkadian 𒌚𒉈 (Abum). Cognate with Hebrew אָב (av, “Av, the fifth month of the Hebrew lunisolar calendar”).

it is used in the manner of a proverb, is of frequent occurrence in poetry, (M,) is said to him who has a father and to him who has not a father, and is an imprecation as to the meaning, of necessity, though enunciative as to the letter; (M, K;) and hence the saying of Jereer,
yaA tayomu tayoma EadiY~K laA A^abaA lakumo
O Teym, Teym of 'Ades, may ye have no

]; which is the strongest evidence of its being a proverb, and not having a literal meaning; for all of [the tribe of] Teym could not have one father, but all of them were fit objects of imprecation and rough speech: (M:) it is an expression of praise: (S:) [i. e.] it is an imprecation against him to whom it is addressed, not, however, said with the desire of its having effect, but on an occasion of intense love, like
, &c.: (Har p. 165:) and sometimes in dispraise, like
: (TA:) or, as A Heyth says, on the authority of Aboo-Sa'eed Ed- Dareer, it expresses the utmost degree of reviling; [meaning
expresses reviling also, but means Thou hast no free, or ingenuous, mother: (Meyd in Har p. 165: [see
for he who has a father relies upon him in some circumstances of his case: (TA:) accord. to Kh, it means
Thou hast none to stand thee in stead of thyself:
(ISh, TA:) Fr says that it is a phrase used by the Arabs [parenthetically, i.e.,] to divide their speech: (TA:) [thus, for instance,] Zufar Ibn-El-Hárith says,

A^ariyniY silaAHiY laA A^abaA laka A_in~aniY

body.text: , and Suleymán put the best construction upon it, [as though it meant,
Send down upon us rain: Thou hast no father
], and said, I testify that He hath no father nor female companion nor offspring. (TA.) They say also, in paying honour [to a person],
May thy hater have no father!
or, accord. to ISk, each is a metonymical expression for
, q. v.) ―         -b4-  One also says, on the occasion of an occurrence that is approved and commended, by way of expressing wonder and praise,
thy father, seeing that he begat thee a generous son, and produced the like of thee!
] it means that to God [alone] belongs the power to create the like of this man [to whom it relates], from whom has proceeded this wonderful action. (Har p. 44.) ―         -b5-  And
She resembles her father in strength of mind,
spirit, and sharpness of disposition, and in hastening,
striving to be first, to do things:
Mayest thou be ransomed with my father!
(see the next sentence but one;) or]
I will ransom thee with my father
shalt be, ransomed with my father
I would ransom thee, with my father

being dependent upon a word suppressed, which, accord. to some, is a [pass. participial] noun, and accord. to others, a verb; and this word is suppressed because of the frequent usage of the phrase. (TA.) You say also,
With my father mayest thou be ransomed, and
May he whom I love be ransomed with my father!
be made a ransom for him
]! (El-Wáhidee on the Deewán of El-Mutanebbee, in De Sacy's Chrest. Arabe, sec. ed. vol. iii. p. 35 of the Arabic text.) Sometimes they change the

waqado zaEamuwA A^an~iY jaziEotu EalayohimaA
                      
                     
                        wahalo jazaEN A^ano qulotu waA biA^abaA humaA
And they have asserted that I have become impatient on account of them two: but is it
impatience that I said, Alas, with my father may they two be ransomed?
. (S.) And some of the Arabs used to say,
Alas, with my father mayest thou be ransomed!
: and hence what is related, in a trad., of Umm- 'Ateeyeh; that she used not to mention the Prophet without saying,
yaA biA^abiY A^anota wa yaA fawoqa Albiyabo
thou to whom I would say,
With my father mayest thou be ransomed! and O
him to whom I would address
the saying, With my father mayest thou be ransomed!

) prefixed to nouns of various significations. Most of the compounds thus formed will be found explained in the arts. to which belong the nouns that occupy the second place. The following are among the more common, and are therefore here mentioned, as exs. of different kinds.] ―         -b11-
(Ibn-Habeeb, M:) it is said in the TS that

to cattle and other beasts, what fruit is to men:
whatever grows upon the face of the earth;

herbage prepared for pasture and for cutting:
because prepared for winter (Bd in lxxx. 31, and Msb) and for journeying: (Msb:) pl. [of pauc.]

entries.lexicon_en: Al-Tahānawī, Kashshāf Iṣṭilāḥāt al-Funūn wa-l-ʿUlūm (d. 1777 CE)
